verse.arabic_text: وَقَالَ رَبُّكُمُ ٱدْعُونِىٓ أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِى سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ دَاخِرِينَ
verse.translation: And your Lord says, 'Call upon Me; I will respond to you.' Indeed, those who disdain My worship will enter Hell [rendered] contemptible.

theme_question.correct: The direct command to make supplication (Dua) with a guarantee of response.
options: The obligation of performing the five daily prayers.
The direct command to make supplication (Dua) with a guarantee of response.
The importance of giving charity to the poor.
The prohibition of associating partners with Allah.

educational_notes.verse_context: This verse comes in Surah Ghafir, also known as Al-Mu'min (The Believer). It is a powerful declaration from Allah Himself, inviting His servants to call upon Him directly. It follows discussions about the signs of Allah's power in creation, highlighting that the Creator is the only One worthy of being called upon.
educational_notes.theme_explanation: The verse establishes a direct and personal connection between the Creator and the creation. The act of 'Dua' (supplication) is presented not just as a request but as an act of worship ('ibadah). The promise 'I will respond to you' is a profound source of hope and comfort for believers. Conversely, refusing to make Dua is portrayed as an act of arrogance (yastakbirun), as it implies a self-sufficiency and a denial of one's need for Allah, which carries a severe warning.
educational_notes.surah_overview: Surah Ghafir (The Forgiver) is the 40th chapter of the Quran. It is a Meccan surah that begins with the Muqatta'at (disjointed letters) Ha, Mim. Its name comes from the mention of Allah as the 'Forgiver of sin' (Ghafir adh-Dhanb) in verse 3. A significant portion of the surah recounts the story of a believing man from the court of Pharaoh who concealed his faith and defended Prophet Musa (Moses), hence the surah's other name, Al-Mu'min (The Believer). The surah emphasizes the Oneness of Allah, the reality of revelation, and the consequences of arrogance and disbelief, contrasting them with the rewards for faith and humility.
